column3: Yarrow thrives in soil that drains well, preventing waterlogged roots.
Plant yarrow in a location with at least 6 hours of direct sunlight daily.
Leave 12-24 inches of space between yarrow plants for healthy growth and air circulation.
Provide regular watering in the first year to establish strong roots.
Plant yarrow in soil that allows excess water to escape, preventing root rot.
Yarrow prefers lean soils; excessive fertilizer can harm the plant.
Remove spent flowers to encourage continuous blooming.
Divide mature plants every 2-3 years to maintain vigor.
Keep an eye out for common pests such as aphids and caterpillars that can harm yarrow.
Prune yarrow in early spring to promote new growth and maintain its shape.
Sweet flag thrives in wet or waterlogged soil, so choose a planting location with good moisture retention.

column2: Provide full sun to part shade
column3: Sweet flag prefers full sun but can tolerate partial shade, so ensure it receives adequate sunlight for healthy growth.
Maintain a consistently moist environment around the plant to mimic its natural habitat, ensuring it never dries out.
When planting multiple sweet flag plants, space them about 12 inches apart to allow room for their growth.
Apply mulch around the base of the plant to help retain soil moisture and protect the root system.
Apply a balanced, slow-release fertilizer in the spring to provide essential nutrients for the growing season.
Remove any dead or damaged leaves to promote a tidy appearance and prevent disease.
Divide mature sweet flag plants every 2-3 years to rejuvenate them and maintain vigorous growth.
Use organic methods like neem oil or beneficial insects to manage common pests without harmful chemicals.
If you wish to propagate sweet flag, harvest rhizomes in the fall and replant or store them for future use.
Ensure the soil allows water to drain easily, preventing waterlogged conditions that can harm the roots.
Maintain a regular watering schedule, avoiding overwatering to prevent root rot and fungal issues.
Choose a location with ample sunlight exposure, as horse chestnuts thrive in full sun conditions.
Apply a layer of mulch around the base to retain soil moisture, suppress weeds, and protect roots from extreme temperatures.
Regularly remove dead or diseased branches to promote overall tree health and prevent the spread of infections.
Apply a balanced fertilizer in the spring to provide essential nutrients for vigorous growth and flowering.
Monitor for pests like caterpillars and aphids, and take prompt action to prevent damage to leaves and buds.
Plant horse chestnuts with sufficient spacing to allow for proper air circulation and avoid overcrowding.
Shield the tree from strong winds, as horse chestnuts are susceptible to wind damage, particularly in exposed locations.
Keep a watchful eye for signs of diseases such as leaf blotch or canker, and take preventive measures or treat promptly if identified.
Garlic is best planted in the fall, allowing it to establish roots before winter.
Ensure proper air circulation by planting garlic bulbs 10-15 cm apart.
Garlic thrives in loose, well-drained soil to prevent rot and fungal issues.
Keep soil consistently moist but avoid waterlogged conditions for healthy garlic growth.
Apply a layer of mulch to insulate soil and regulate temperature during winter.
Garlic requires minimal fertilizer; excessive nitrogen can hinder bulb development.
Trim garlic scapes to redirect energy for larger bulb formation.
Harvest when the lower leaves turn yellow, signaling bulb maturity.
Allow harvested bulbs to dry in a well-ventilated area for a few weeks.
Store cured garlic bulbs in a cool, dry location to prevent sprouting and maintain quality.
